session_1.user_goal: The general idea of my action in this case was to avoid unfairness and yet still save the friendship. I also wanted to make sure that the financial agreement between myself and the client was honored and yet, I did not want the matter to hurt our relationship or trust. It was not the money in the end, but a sense of being respected, heard and having a healthy and honest relationship.
session_1.scenario:
An instance that I tend to think about is when I had a fallout with my best friend over money. This was during a period of closeness with each other and we shared almost everything including minor financial commitments whenever we spend together. The dispute started when we decided to make a short trip and decided to divide the costs equally. This further cost me the majority of the cost since I had negotiated with my friend that I would pay most of the costs now and be paid later. But at the moment of need there were hesitations and delays, and this brought about a strain with me.
I did not handle the problem peacefully and allowed it to frustrate me. I was made to feel used and I started interpreting what my friend had done as inconsiderate and careless. I chose not to engage in a conversation and turn emotional instead. I was withdrawn, cold-tempered, and did not want to spend time together. This later on resulted in an argument where we both said what we did in anger. My friend claimed that I had not been specific enough with regards to my expectations and pressured them, thus I was accusing her of being irresponsible with her money.
In retrospect, I understand that one of my main weaknesses was the failure to communicate effectively in the outset. I thought that my friend could realize how urgent it was to repay me, however, I never placed any timeline or said that it was that important to me. The other bad choice was to put emotions in charge rather than be patient and understanding to the situation. My response caused the conflict to be more personal than it had to be by making a financial misunderstanding escalate into a further strain on our friendship.
I would have managed the situation in a different way by having an open and peaceful conversation in the beginning. Had I explicitly stated what I expected and allowed my friend to hear me out we would not have to have the kind of tension we had. I also feel bad that I had not thought of the fact that my friend could have been going through his/her own financial troubles at that time.
This made me understand the value of communication particularly in case it involves money. It taught me that even powerful relationships may be spoiled by misconceptions in the case they are not dealt with in an appropriate manner. Given an event of the same scenario, I would ensure that I focus more on clarity, empathy, and straight talk to avoid worsening of the situation.
User_Plans_Final: I could have asked about my friend’s situation and listened without assuming
I could have come up with a clear repayment plat ealrier.

LLM-CT.item: I could have agreed on a clear repayment amount and deadline with my friend before the trip, and then calmly checked in as soon as the payment was delayed so we could address any problem early without letting resentment damage the friendship.

LLM-CF.item: I could have calmly brought up the repayment as soon as the delay started and clearly said when I needed my friend to pay me back, while asking if they were having any financial difficulty so we could agree on a fair timeline before resentment built up.

LLM-B.item: I could have had a calm, honest conversation at the start and clearly explained when I needed the money repaid instead of assuming my friend understood my expectations.

session_2.user_goal: My general aim was to have my mum comprehend and contribute to my project with insecurities instead of being discouraged. I also wanted both of us to talk in a relaxed way as I would be able to work on my goals with her support and assurance.
session_2.scenario:
A couple of months back, I was in an infuriating fight with my mum concerning her assistance in something that is significant to me. I was doing a personal project which needed emotional support as well as some practical assistance and I felt that she was reactionary and detached. I remember that morning perfectly: I went up to her with enthusiasm and told her what I had already done and what I wanted to do. I wanted to be reassured or even given a feeling that she knew how hard I was trying. Rather, she hastily discounted my ideas citing that it was too ambitious or that I needed to work on more practical things. Her words left me feeling misunderstood and undervalued and rather than talk calmly about my emotions, I responded defensively. I also shouted and indicated that it was like she did not believe in me because she was not supporting me. This argument got out of control when I took her practical issues as personal attack and she claimed that she was just trying to save me the disappointment.
In retrospect, I should note that my response made the situation worse. I allowed my feelings to overpower me, and I was only trying to make her feel wrong rather than showing why it was important to me that she encouraged me. I did not take time to think about her viewpoint and how she could have been warned and this could have led to the argument not escalating. After the shouting had ceased a kind of awkward silence ensued, and I withdrew with a hurt and unrelated feeling, and she appeared to be frustrated and remote. This strain persisted in a few days, so it was hard to approach the subject matter once again and request assistance.
There are a number of ways in which I could have dealt with the situation at that time. I would have been more relaxed in the discussion, not accusing her but stating the feelings. I could have sought her opinion and made an effort to know what she was worried about instead of assuming that she was indifferent. I might also have proposed a middle ground, requesting minor, feasible assistance to her and at the same time counter her warning. Such moves would have enabled us to engage in a fruitful discussion, as opposed to frustration creating a rift between us. When looking back at this experience, I have come to the realization that communication, patience, and empathy are essential in solving disagreements, particularly one who has provided support to me and whose support is important. By being more creative in the way I approach these conversations, I would prefer the next conflict to be shaped into an opportunity of understanding and not a hurt.
User_Plans_Final: I would have swallowed my pride as mums always have the best intensions for us

LLM-CT.item: I could have started the conversation by calmly telling my mum that this project meant a lot to me and that before discussing practical concerns, I really needed her to first hear what I had done and give me some encouragement, then ask what worries she had so we could find a small way she could help.

LLM-CF.item: I could have paused when she first called my project too ambitious and calmly said, \"I know you’re worried, but I really need encouragement first—can I show you what I’ve done and then hear your concerns?\"

LLM-B.item: I could have calmly told my mum that I needed encouragement first and then asked about her concerns so we could find a small way for her to support my project without turning the conversation into an argument.

session_1.user_goal: My overall goal was to ensure the software project stayed on schedule while maintaining a professional and collaborative relationship with my colleague, so that we could deliver a high-quality product without unnecessary conflict.
session_1.scenario:
A few months ago, I encountered a challenging situation at work while collaborating with a colleague on a software project. We were responsible for developing different modules of a new application, and I was counting on my teammate to deliver their part on time so we could integrate everything smoothly. As the deadline approached, my colleague missed several check-ins and submitted incomplete code, which caused delays and frustration on my end. When I addressed the issue, I let my frustration show, raising my voice and expressing my disappointment more harshly than intended. Instead of finding a solution, the conversation became tense, and my colleague became defensive. Looking back, I realize that my assumptions and emotional response only worsened the situation and created unnecessary tension in the team.

My goal was to communicate my concerns calmly, ensure the project stayed on track, and maintain a professional and cooperative relationship with my colleague. In hindsight, I should have approached the conversation differently by pausing to reflect, actively listening to their perspective, and focusing on collaborative problem-solving. This experience taught me the importance of managing emotions, practicing empathy, and communicating constructively in a professional setting. If I could replay the moment, I would remain calm, understand my colleague’s challenges, and propose a solution that supports both of our contributions, ensuring a better outcome for the project and the team dynamic.
User_Plans_Final: Take a few moments before responding to my colleague to manage my frustration and avoid reacting impulsively.
Ask my colleague if there were challenges or obstacles causing the delays, rather than assuming negligencee

LLM-CT.item: I could have scheduled a calm one-on-one conversation as soon as I noticed the missed check-ins, asked my colleague what was blocking their progress, and worked with them to agree on a revised plan with clear milestones so we could keep the project on track without escalating tension.

LLM-CF.item: I could have paused before confronting my colleague and addressed the missed check-ins in a calm one-on-one conversation by asking what was blocking their progress and agreeing on a concrete plan to complete the remaining code and integration steps.

LLM-B.item: I could have paused before confronting my colleague and calmly asked about any obstacles they were facing so we could work together on a plan to keep the project on schedule.

My partner became defensive, and the conversation quickly turned tense, leaving both of us upset. session_1.user_goal: My goal in this situation was to share my concerns about the project in a calm and helpful way while keeping a positive relationship with my team and making sure the project stayed on track.
session_1.scenario:
A few months ago, I was in a team meeting at work. We were discussing an important project and trying to decide how to move forward. One of my colleagues suggested an idea that I didn’t agree with. Instead of staying calm, I reacted quickly and emotionally. I interrupted them and pointed out what I thought was wrong with their plan.
I was stressed because I had many tasks to finish, and I felt pressure to get the project done on time. My frustration made me speak louder and act more aggressively than I intended. Other people in the meeting looked uncomfortable, and my colleague seemed upset. I realized later that my behavior didn’t help solve the problem and made the team feel tense.
Looking back, I see that I could have handled it differently. I could have paused before speaking, listened carefully to their ideas, and then suggested my own ideas in a polite, constructive way. I could have asked questions to understand their reasoning instead of just pointing out mistakes. This would have helped the team work together better and keep the meeting productive.
User_Plans_Final: Take a moment to pause and calm down before speaking.
Let my colleague finish explaining their idea without interrupting.
Listen carefully to understand their point of view
Share my concerns in a calm and polite way.
Ask questions or suggest other options to work together.
Keep a positive tone so the team feels comfortable.
Think afterward about what worked and what I could do better next time.

LLM-CT.item: I could have paused before responding, let my colleague finish explaining their idea, and then calmly said, \"I have some concerns about that approach—could I share them and ask a few questions so we can compare options?\"

LLM-CF.item: I could have paused before responding, let my colleague finish explaining their idea, and then calmly said, \"I’m worried about how this might affect the timeline—could we talk through that part?\"

LLM-B.item: I could have paused before responding, listened fully to my colleague’s idea, and then shared my concerns calmly and constructively.

session_2.user_goal: My goal in this situation was to share my ideas about organizing the holiday in a way that included everyone and reduced stress, while keeping the conversation respectful and collaborative.
session_2.scenario:
A few months ago, I was at a family dinner talking about plans for an upcoming holiday. I wanted to share my ideas to make the event enjoyable and include everyone. But one of my relatives suggested a plan that I didn’t agree with because I thought it would be stressful for some family members.
Instead of staying calm, I reacted quickly. I interrupted them, pointed out problems with their plan, and tried to convince everyone my idea was better. My voice got louder, and some family members looked uncomfortable. Others started defending the original plan, and the conversation turned into an argument.
Looking back, I could have handled the situation differently. I could have paused before speaking, listened to my relative’s idea without interrupting, and asked questions to understand their perspective. I could have pointed out the good parts of their plan and calmly explained my concerns. I also could have suggested compromises or alternative solutions in a polite and collaborative way.
During the conversation, I let my frustration and stress affect how I spoke. I focused on being “right” instead of working together to create a plan everyone could enjoy. If I had acted differently, the discussion could have been more productive, and the family could have come up with a plan that included everyone. This experience taught me the importance of staying calm, listening carefully, and sharing concerns politely.

Let my relative finish explaining their idea without interrupting.
Listen carefully to understand their perspective.
Share my concerns politely and calmly.
Suggest compromises or alternative options that include everyone.
Acknowledge the positive parts of their plan.
Keep a friendly tone and body language to make the conversation comfortable.
Reflect afterward on what went well and what I could improve next time.

LLM-CT.item: I could have paused before responding to my relative’s suggestion, listened without interrupting, and then calmly said, \"I like that your plan brings everyone together—could we also think about how to make it less stressful for some family members?\"

session_1.user_goal: My overall goal was to do get a good grade on the project.
session_1.scenario:
A scenario I tend to consider took place when I was doing a group project in a science course. There were a few weeks to make a presentation and at the beginning I realized that there was no uniform communication between group members. Other individuals were not responding to the group chat and time was already slipping away. Rather than responding to the problem, I decided to take up more work myself to keep the project on track. I reread parts of the presentation, did missing slides and did not bring up issues since I did not want to cause conflict and sound domineering.

The unequal contribution was even more evident as the deadline was approaching. In our last preparation, one of the members of our group was not conversant with the content that they were supposed to deliver and this influenced the general quality and smooth flow. In spite of the fact we finished the project, it did not correspond to the work I was expecting to achieve, and I was frustrated by the fact I had assumed so much responsibility without enhancing the coordination of the team.

In retrospect, I would have done more to manage the situation. They should have started the discussion regarding expectations, roles, and deadlines at an earlier stage. I would have proposed more regular check-ins, or even breaking down tasks in a more visible way to achieve accountability. It would have been uncomfortable to address the issue directly at the time but probably it would have ameliorated the group dynamics and the end result.

The experience provided me with the lesson on the role of communication, boundary-setting, and leadership in teamwork. I also got to know that sometimes it is better to fight until the last drop of blood and that a successful teamwork involves responsibility and candor.
User_Plans_Final: Reach out to members earlier.
Ask the teacher for help.

session_1.user_goal: To maintain professional composure and set firm, data-backed boundaries under extreme stress without sabotaging my long-term career standing.
session_1.scenario: Three years ago, while leading a high-pressure digital launch, my manager Marcus unilaterally added massive new features to our project scope just three weeks before our deadline. Exhausted from months of 14-hour days, I snapped during a team meeting when he questioned my competence in front of my peers. Instead of pulling him aside to discuss the technical impossibility of his request, I chose to launch into a heated, public tirade, listing every one of his past failures before grabbing my laptop and walking out for good. My decision to quit on the spot felt like a victory in the moment, but it ultimately cost me my project bonus, abandoned my team during a crisis, and severely tarnished my professional reputation within a small, tight-knit industry. I realized too late that by letting my emotions drive my exit, I had traded a decade of career building for five minutes of catharsis, leaving me to spend the next two years rebuilding my credibility from scratch.
User_Plans_Final: In future high-pressure conflicts, I will move the discussion from a public forum to a private, data-driven meeting. I will focus on documenting the impact of decisions on project goals rather than reacting to personal provocations, ensuring I protect both my professional reputation and my team’s workload.

LLM-CT.item: I could have stopped the meeting the moment Marcus questioned my competence, said I wanted to address scope and feasibility offline, and then met with him immediately afterward with a written impact estimate showing what the new features would cost in time, staffing, and launch risk while proposing a phased plan and documenting the decision.

LLM-CF.item: I could have stopped myself from responding in the meeting, said that adding major features three weeks before launch was not technically feasible without changing scope or timeline, and asked Marcus to discuss the tradeoffs with me privately right after so I could present a concrete impact assessment instead of quitting in anger.

LLM-B.item: I could have calmly requested a private conversation with Marcus immediately after the meeting and presented a clear, data-backed assessment of the added features’ impact on timeline, workload, and delivery risk, while firmly stating what was realistically achievable before the deadline instead of reacting publicly.

session_2.user_goal: To replace emotional, fear-based financial decision-making with a "calculated risk" framework that allows for growth while maintaining a reasonable safety net.
session_2.scenario: In late 2019, I had saved $15,000 in seed money when a close colleague, Sarah, approached me to be an early investor in her eco-friendly packaging startup. Despite having a solid business plan and a clear market gap, I allowed a "scarcity mindset" and a paralyzed fear of risk to dictate my decision, ultimately choosing to keep my funds in a low-interest savings account for "safety" rather than conducting a proper risk assessment. I gave her a vague, non-committal response until she eventually secured funding elsewhere, and I watched from the sidelines as the e-commerce boom of 2020 caused her company’s valuation to triple in less than two years. By choosing the perceived security of a stagnant bank account over a calculated investment in a field I understood, I didn't just lose out on a significant financial windfall; I damaged a professional relationship and realized that my inability to distinguish between "gambling" and "calculated risk" was holding my future self hostage. I spent the next several years regretting the missed seat at the table, realizing that my "safe" choice was actually the riskiest move I could have made because it resulted in 100% certain stagnation.
User_Plans_Final: To better achieve my goal, I could have committed to a strict two-week deadline to review the business plan and financials, rather than letting the decision linger indefinitely due to anxiety. I could have also calculated a "maximum tolerable loss"—a specific dollar amount like $3,000—that I could afford to lose without jeopardizing my core safety net, allowing me to invest with confidence. Finally, I could have sought an objective third-party audit or market analysis to replace my internal fear with data-driven evidence, which would have shown the high growth potential of the sustainable packaging industry at that time.

In the future, when faced with a high-stakes opportunity, I will replace emotional procrastination with a structured 14-day risk assessment and a pre-defined "capped" investment strategy that preserves my financial security while ensuring I never again let fear of the unknown result in total stagnation.

LLM-CT.item: I could have told Sarah I was interested but needed two weeks to do a structured risk assessment, then reviewed her financials, defined a maximum loss I could tolerate, and decided in advance to invest only a capped portion of my $15,000—such as $5,000—while keeping the rest as an emergency buffer instead of defaulting to fear and leaving all of it in savings.

LLM-CF.item: I could have committed to a defined risk-assessment process and invested a small, pre-set portion of my $15,000—such as $3,000 to $5,000—in Sarah’s startup only after reviewing her business plan, market assumptions, and downside scenarios, instead of avoiding the decision and keeping all the money in savings out of fear.

LLM-B.item: I could have set a clear deadline to review Sarah’s business plan, assess the downside against my safety-net needs, and decide whether to invest a defined portion of my $15,000 based on a calculated risk instead of avoiding the decision out of fear.

session_3.user_goal: To establish firm "no-fly zones" around my professional and personal milestones, transitioning from an "emergency responder" role to a "supportive consultant" role in family dynamics.
session_3.scenario: A few years back, I was stuck in this exhausting loop of being the "fixer" for my younger brother, who couldn't seem to keep his life together. I thought being a good sibling meant jumping into the fire every time he messed up, but I was really just a human safety net. The breaking point hit when I had a massive, career-defining certification exam on a Monday—something I’d poured six months of my life and thousands of dollars into. That Saturday, he got evicted and called me panicking, needing a four-hour drive and a weekend of heavy lifting to move his stuff. I felt that familiar, heavy guilt and chose him over myself. I spent the whole weekend hauling furniture, got home at 3:00 AM Monday, and walked into that exam a total zombie. I failed by a hair, losing a promotion and a year’s worth of progress, while he was right back in another crisis six months later anyway. My decision didn't "save" him; it just dragged me down with him and sparked a resentment that poisoned our relationship for years, teaching me the hard way that you can’t light yourself on fire to keep someone else warm.
User_Plans_Final: In the future, when a loved one's avoidable crisis clashes with a major professional milestone, I will prioritize my own success by setting a firm boundary on my physical presence and offering limited, remote guidance instead of direct intervention.

LLM-CT.item: I could have told my brother that I would not spend the weekend moving him because of my Monday certification exam, and instead limited my help to a short phone call helping him contact other movers, friends, or storage options after my exam was over.

LLM-CF.item: I could have told my brother on Saturday that I could not spend the weekend moving him because of my Monday certification exam, and limited my help to a brief call helping him find other immediate options like friends, a rental truck, or temporary storage.

LLM-B.item: I could have told my brother that because of my career-defining exam, I was not available to drive out and rescue him that weekend, and instead offered limited support from a distance by helping him call movers, storage facilities, or other local resources.

session_1.user_goal: My main goal was to make sure our team's final project proposal included a wide range of ideas and perspectives, making it as thorough and innovative as possible. I wanted to help create a high-quality result that represented everyone's input while encouraging team members to share their views comfortably. Additionally, I aimed to improve my communication skills by speaking up assertively so important ideas weren't missed, which would benefit both the project's success and my personal growth in teamwork.
session_1.scenario:
A few years back, I worked with my team at my old job on a project to create a new marketing plan for an important product. We had many brainstorming sessions, analyzed data, and developed strategies. My role was to present our final proposal to the company's senior management.

As the project went on, I saw that some team members seemed reluctant to share their ideas. They might have been worried about being criticized or thought their opinions wouldn't matter. I felt it was important for everyone to communicate openly and contribute different perspectives in order to build a strong, innovative plan. So, during meetings, I tried to encourage quieter members by asking questions that allowed them more freedom so they would feel comfortable sharing what they thought.

As we got closer to the final presentation, I started getting worried that we hadn't included some important viewpoints in our plan. I realized certain ideas, especially from quieter team members, were missing. This made me think our proposal might not be strong and could hurt how management sees us. Even though I was concerned about this, I held back because I didn't want to disturb the team's harmony or seem too critical.

On the day of the presentation, I shared our proposal confidently but felt it could have been better. Later, in a meeting with my manager, I realized that if I'd spoken up more strongly before finalizing everything, we could've fixed some weak areas. In hindsight, I wish I'd pushed harder to include those missed ideas, even if it meant facing disagreements or having longer discussions.

This experience taught me that it's important to speak up and support different opinions when working with others. I learned that trying too hard to avoid conflict or keep everyone happy can lead to poor results. It made me understand the need to be both polite and firm, while appreciating everyone's contributions, even if they seem unsure at first.

If I could go back, I'd handle things differently. I would have spoken up sooner and with more confidence while we were planning so that everyone's ideas got heard. Maybe I’d suggest a special session just for brainstorming before picking the best options. Plus, I'd highlight how important it is to hear different viewpoints and create an atmosphere where everyone felt safe sharing their thoughts without being judged.

To sum up, this experience was key for me to grow in teamwork and communication. It taught me how important it is to be confident, include everyone, and support different ideas early on. Even though the outcome wasn't perfect, it pushed me to improve my communication skills and appreciate what each person can add to a project.
User_Plans_Final: Express Concerns Sooner: I should have spoken up about the missing viewpoints earlier in the project instead of waiting until the final presentation.
Encourage Open Dialogue: I could have made the discussions more inclusive by directly asking quieter team members to share their ideas.
Propose setting up a meeting just for brainstorming, where everyone can share their ideas before deciding on the best ones. This way, everyone's thoughts are taken into account.
Highlight the Importance of Diversity: Explain to the team how different viewpoints can make a proposal stronger and more creative, and encourage an open-minded atmosphere.
Take notes of all ideas during meetings, especially from quieter participants. Review these notes later to make sure they're included in the final plan.

LLM-CT.item: I could have scheduled a dedicated brainstorming session before finalizing the proposal and explicitly asked each team member, especially the quieter ones, to share their ideas so we could evaluate and include the strongest missing perspectives before the presentation.

LLM-CF.item: I could have spoken up before the proposal was finalized and explicitly suggested a short additional brainstorming discussion to bring in the quieter team members’ ideas and address the missing perspectives.

LLM-B.item: I could have spoken up earlier and more assertively to make sure quieter team members’ ideas were included before we finalized the proposal.

Lastly, some of my materials were either incomplete or outdated since I hurried through checking everything at the end.

Even though there were warning signs, I didn't act early to fix the problems. Instead, I hoped things would work out at the last minute. I thought that a rush on the event day could save us. So, I spent the night before trying urgently to confirm where we’d meet and remind speakers while printing materials in a hurry. Even though I was nervous and stressed, I told myself that it would all be enough for a good event. which delayed the start time. Some speakers weren't prepared because they didn't get final schedules. The printed materials were incomplete and had mistakes, confusing everyone there. In short, it was a messy event that did not live up to my expectations.

Reflecting on the past, I'm sorry I didn't plan or communicate better. I wish I'd double-checked all bookings sooner, kept in touch with speakers often, and gone over materials more carefully. Creating a detailed project timeline with deadlines would have helped keep things on track ahead of time. Also, delegating tasks to team members and following up regularly could have made everything run smoother.

I now understand that I should have talked openly with my supervisor and team about the problems I was having. Rather than trying to handle everything by myself, I could have asked for help, changed deadlines, or shared some tasks with others. This would have lowered my stress and let me concentrate on important things, making the event better overall.

From this experience, I learned how crucial it is to plan early, communicate clearly, and review everything carefully. It showed me that hurrying and being too confident can cause unnecessary errors. Since then, I've worked hard to plan better, stay in touch with my team often, and check all details before starting major projects. Although things didn’t go as planned at the time, it turned into a valuable lesson that has helped me become more organized and efficient in my work.
User_Plans_Final: Plan early: Start your planning process ahead of time, so you have enough opportunity to coordinate and make any necessary changes.
Make a Detailed Project Timeline: Create a complete schedule with clear deadlines for each task, such as booking locations, finalizing speakers, and getting materials ready.
Check Bookings and Plans: Regularly make sure venue bookings, speaker agreements, and other logistical plans are in order to avoid any mix-ups or mistakes.
Set Up Regular Meetings: Arrange regular follow-ups with team members, vendors, and speakers to monitor progress and quickly resolve any problems.
Create a Main Checklist: Use this list to keep track of every task, responsibility, and deadline so you don't miss anything.

LLM-CT.item: I could have created a detailed project timeline at the start of the seminar planning, with clear deadlines for venue confirmation, speaker check-ins, and material reviews, and then used it to track progress each week so problems were caught and fixed before the event.

LLM-CF.item: I could have created and used a simple checklist with firm deadlines one week before the seminar to confirm the venue booking, follow up with each speaker for final presentations and schedules, and review all printed materials before the last minute.

LLM-B.item: I could have created a detailed project timeline with clear deadlines and regular check-ins to track bookings, speaker preparation, and materials well before the event.

feedback.1: What I like about using the system:  It offers clear guidance to help users organize their ideas and create thorough answers.  It can help you come up with, plan, and improve ideas quickly.  The system provides regular, fair help that can be useful for understanding and concentration.  It's always available, so users can get help anytime they need it.  What I don't like or see as drawbacks:  It might miss the subtle understanding that humans have, especially in tricky emotional situations or those needing attention to context.  Sometimes, it might not completely understand personal experiences unless you provide detailed information.  It depends on the details given; if the input is unclear or missing information, the answers might not fit as well.  It might not always understand complex relationships or cultural differences.  When this system would be helpful:  When someone needs help sorting out their ideas or making plans.  To help with learning how to organize answers or come up with ideas.  As a partner to come up with new ideas or options.  When trying to find a fair and impartial view on an issue.  To practice communication or writing skills.  When help is needed after normal work hours.

They often offer various choices, step-by-step guidance, and questions that encourage reflection, which can assist in clarifying ideas and finding areas for improvement.  These suggestions can help shape my thinking by pointing out things I might not have thought about before. They encourage me to try different ideas or think more deeply. They also push me to review my choices, focus on good practices, and consider results more carefully. feedback.3: Personalization and Context Awareness: It would grasp the user's individual situation, background, and goals to customize suggestions based on their specific needs, likes, and values.  Interactive Dialogue: It allows for real-time, back-and-forth conversations where users can ask more questions, clarify advice, and look at different perspectives.  Different Views: The system would show a variety of choices, highlighting possible benefits, risks, and compromises for each action. This helps in making well-rounded decisions.  Visual and Multi-Modal Support: It would include visual tools like charts, diagrams, or decision trees to help users see and understand complex decisions and their outcomes.  Scenario Simulation: Being able to mimic possible outcomes based on various actions would help users see what might happen before they decide. Some examples of helpful interactions are:  Using open-ended questions to look into different choices.  Asking for different situations and results.  List the good and bad points for each suggested action.  Getting brief overviews or in-depth descriptions.  Using diagrams or timelines to show possible outcomes.  Keeping a record of decisions for later use.  Getting gentle reminders to think about things we might have missed or any biases.  An ideal AI for brainstorming and decision-making should be flexible, easy to interact with, open about its processes, and help people make confident decisions in tricky situations.

session_1.user_goal: My goal in reacting the way I did was to let my mother know that I was not going to be silent and allow her to badmouth me over a situation she had no business being involved in.  I wanted to know that her behavior was not ok.
session_1.scenario: I was with my family, and we were meeting to drive from NY to CT to visit a farm near my brother's house as part of a family outing.  We all met at my mom's apartment building in NY, including my household, my aunt and uncle, and my dad (my parents are separated).  After waiting over an hour for my mom and sister to come downstairs, I called the phone in my mom's apartment. My sister answered, and we spoke for about a minute with me expressing frustration that we were waiting so long and asking what was going on.  My sister never gave me a clear answer, but mid- conversation, the phone went dead.  I assumed my sister hung up the phone on me, which I found incredibly disrespectful (my sister is 19 years younger than me so I practically raised her and I am like a second mother to her). I called back and asked my sister if she hung up the phone on me, and she sucked her teeth and said no, but then a few seconds later, after I said something she didn't like, the phone went dead again. Furious, I called back, and no one answered the phone.  About 10 minutes later, my mom and sister finally came downstairs, and without speaking we all started to move towards our cars.  Then, I heard my mom loudly badmouthing me and telling my relatives how I was yelling at my sister and starting some senseless argument, and didn't know what I was talking about. My sister is the baby of the family, and there is a lot of favoritism there, with my mom being very protective of her. Hearing my mom loudly badmouthing me when I was standing just feet away just triggered me, and I completely lost my cool.  Midway to my car, I turned around and charged towards my mother, yelling and cussing at her that she had no business inserting herself into a dispute between my sister and I, and yelling that if she had something to say about me, she ought to say it to my face, not run her mouth to other people.  As soon as she saw me charge towards her, my mother hurried to the car she was riding in and tried to avoid confrontation with me, but that just angered me even more because she has a habit of starting situations, then cowering and acting like she did nothing wrong when they escalate. After about a minute or so of yelling at her and her refusing to get out of the car, my uncle pulled me away, and I started walking back to my car, still cussing loudly at her and extremely pissed off.  When we got into my uncle's car, my husband then turned to me and said "I would never speak to my mother that way" and I almost lost it again.  I had the composure to just say to him "please don't compare me and my mother to you and your mother because we are not the same."  However, I was deeply hurt that he wasn't the least bit concerned about me. The moment seemed like it was about a simple argument with my sister but it was much deeper - a reaction to years of being bullied and mistreated by my mother and expected to stay silent.  The fact that my husband couldn't see that and was judging me in that moment, rather than being a support system for me hurt just as much so I stayed silent the entire drive to CT and didn't speak to anyone.  When we arrived, I did apologize to my sister and made amends with my mom.
User_Plans_Final: Not said anything
Waited to speak to my mom later and in private
Waited until I was calm and speak to my mom later in public
Spoke loudly and firmly but not charged at her, telling her that her behavior was not ok
Walked over to her calmly and expressed my feelings calmly

Calmly expressed how I felt in the moment, then set up a separate time after the fact to discuss the deeply rooted issues that led me to want to charge at her

session_2.user_goal: My goal was to end the relationship as soon as I knew there was infidelity
session_2.scenario: I had been dating a guy for about 9 years off and on, but mostly on.  Things were pretty serious between us and I was living with him in his apartment.  During my birthday weekend, I agreed to go on a last minute three day road trip with my boyfriend, his mom, and his aunt to visit another aunt in South Carolina who was in the hospital. During the trip, while we were in the hotel room, a woman called my boyfriend's phone while he was in the shower and when I told him about it later, he acted like it was his alarm and claimed no one had called him. Later that same evening another female called his phone and he quickly declined the call but not before I could see that it was a female and not the same female from earlier. I asked more questions and he became more evasive.  When I asked him to give me his phone if he had nothing to hide, he refused, but when we got back home, then he offered to show me the phone.  Of course, by then his entire call history was erased. I immediately knew he was cheating but for some reason I didn't break up with him  then- I felt like I needed more concrete evidence.  A few weeks later, while I was at work, my best friend texted me to tell me that a coworker of hers claimed to have been out on a date with my boyfriend two nights earlier when he told me he was meeting up with a former classmate about a business opportunity.  This woman claimed she had been dating him for several months.  Apparently, during their meetup, my best friend's name came up because she lived in his town, which is not very big. My boyfriend mentioned that he knew her but wouldn't say how, which caused the female to be suspicious, so she reached out to my best friend directly since they worked together.  That's when my best friend informed her that he had been in a relationship with me "forever." My gut told me she wasn't lying but again, I felt like I needed more evidence.  I asked the woman if she would speak to me directly and after some hesitation she finally agreed.  She told me all about their history of dating and shared details about his apartment, his bedroom, and other details that indicated she wasn't lying or making it up.  I called my boyfriend but he wouldn't answer the phone which made me delirious.  Not being able to deal with all the emotions I was feeling I drove to his house but even though his car was in his parking spot, he acted like he wasn't home, turned off all his lights and wouldn't answer the door. I staked out his apartment that night for hours, then finally drove to my best friends house and drove home in the morning.  I even worked with the woman to try to set him up by listening in one of their phone conversations but I think she suspected something so the conversation was short.  It wasn't until four days later that he finally called me and I broke down crying and broke up with him.  I was upset with myself for not doing it much sooner.

session_3.user_goal: My goal was to do a great job at the new company and be successful as I had with my prior companies but that did not seem possible. Ultimately I should have exited.
session_3.scenario: I was recruited by a company's CMO to work for a company that had a really great reputation for diversity and inclusion, and that was well respected in my industry.  The company was doing well and it seemed like a no brainer to leave the job I was in, which was struggling and where I didn't feel I had job security, to pursue this opportunity.  However, from the very beginning there were red flags.  First, my new manager requested a call with me a week before I joined to "discuss the team." During this call she outright told me that one of the team members were going to be a problem for me because they were in the role before me but was too junior, and were upset that the company hired someone more senior and took the role from them.  This was a red flag to me because I had experienced a similar situation at a prior company, where I was considered the "problem employee" and my new manager at that company was "warned" that I was going to be difficult all because I asked why I had not been considered for the position.  I had seen the damage that type of behavior could do to someone's career unfairly so I plainly told my new manager in this role that I appreciated her sharing these "insights" but that I would like to meet the team and form my own opinions based on my own experiences.  The next major red flag was about a month into the role when I was asked to be on a hiring loop for a position on a neighboring team. The VP insisted I join the loop even after I flagged that I did not think I would be an impartial interviewer because I had gone through training with one of the candidates.  I felt that she wanted me there because I'm African American and she wanted the company to seem more diverse than it really was. During the interview debrief, myself and an Asian leader (the rest of the loop consisted of white women) indicated that one of the candidates who had been operating as a temp in the position (a white woman) was ill equipped for the role, while another candidate who happened to be African American, was highly impressive.  Our feedback was ignored and the preferred white woman was hired.  She was let go about a month later when it became painfully obvious that she could not handle the role.  When I expressed my concerns to HR about this, I was interrupted mid sentence then interrogated about whether I had been talking to other people about the situation and whether others had been filling my head with information about this.  Then when I expressed my concerns about the conversation with HR to my manager, her response was "well I've worked with her for years and she's great, so maybe give her the benefit of the doubt."  The next major red flag was a few months later, I took two days off for my birthday.  When I returned to work, I was told that the CMO made an executive decision that negated the work I had done the prior 3-4 months on an initiative I was leading.  An output of that initiative was a recommendation to do one thing, and he told the team to do the exact opposite.  At that point I knew that the company was not a fit for me and that I was not likely to be successful there, but I was two months out from closing on my home purchase and couldn't afford to quit so I stayed.  The next two and a half years just got worse.
User_Plans_Final: Withdrawn my offer and asked for my old job back at the first sign of trouble
After the bias hiring incident, started looking for another job
After the CMO override incident, started looking for another job
Waited until the closing on my home, then begun an executive job search
Given it a year to maintain resume integrity, then began looking for a new job
Immediately start networking at the first red flag sign, and leveraging my network to find a new position

LLM-CT.item: I should have treated the pre-start warning from my new manager and the biased hiring incident as decisive signals, paused my home purchase, and started a confidential exit plan immediately after the CMO overrode my work—quietly networking, applying elsewhere, and setting a firm deadline to leave within a few months instead of staying for the next two and a half years.

LLM-CF.item: I should have started a confidential external job search and planned my exit as soon as the CMO overrode my core initiative a few months in, then left shortly after my home closing instead of staying another two and a half years in a company where the red flags already showed I was unlikely to succeed.

LLM-B.item: I could have started a discreet exit plan as soon as the pattern of red flags became clear, while preserving my financial runway through the home purchase, instead of staying and hoping the environment would improve.

session_1.user_goal: My primary objective in this situation was to provide the client with an accurate and comprehensive report, ensuring all details were correct and findings reliable. This was crucial for upholding professional credibility and meeting project expectations.
session_1.scenario:
A few months prior, I was engaged in a significant project at my workplace that required collaboration with various teams to produce a detailed report for a client. The project was critical, characterized by stringent deadlines and considerable expectations from both my team and the client. My role involved collecting data, analyzing it, and synthesizing the results into a presentation that was to be delivered to the client by the end of the week.

As the deadline loomed, I observed that some data from one of the essential departments was delayed. Rather than addressing this issue proactively at an early stage, I attempted to navigate around the absent data by making assumptions and filling in the gaps with estimates. I was confident in my ability to manage the situation and still meet the deadline. I dedicated additional hours to assembling everything, frequently working late and sacrificing my personal time.

Nevertheless, during the final review, it became apparent that the data I had interpolated was incorrect, resulting in flawed conclusions in the report. When I presented the findings to my manager, it was evident that the report required substantial revisions. This led to a hurried rework, a missed deadline, and a dissatisfied client. The situation created stress for all parties involved and adversely affected my team's credibility.

Reflecting on the experience, I wish I had approached the situation differently. Instead of attempting to mask the missing data or hastily conducting the analysis, I should have communicated the delays promptly to my manager and the pertinent departments. By doing so, I could have coordinated a strategy to acquire the missing data or modified the project scope and timeline as necessary. This would have facilitated a more accurate and comprehensive report, ultimately resulting in a more favorable outcome for the client and reduced stress for my team.

The primary decision I regret was failing to prioritize transparency and proactive communication. I believed I could manage the issue discreetly and avoid alarming stakeholders, but in doing so, I compromised the quality of my work and the trust that others had in my abilities.
User_Plans_Final: Immediately Notify My Manager
Contact the Delayed Department
Request a Revised Timeline
Prioritize Transparency
Collaborate with Colleagues
Prepare a Contingency Plan

LLM-CT.item: I could have immediately informed my manager and the delayed department that the missing data put the report at risk, and worked with them to secure the correct figures or formally adjust the timeline before continuing the analysis.

LLM-CF.item: I could have immediately told my manager and the delayed department that the missing data would compromise the report, and requested either the required data or a revised timeline before proceeding with the analysis.

LLM-B.item: I could have immediately informed my manager and the relevant teams about the missing data so we could adjust the timeline or scope and ensure the report was based on accurate information.

session_3.user_goal: My primary objective in this scenario was to launch a focused and impactful marketing campaign aimed at boosting brand awareness and generating leads, all while ensuring that it was informed by precise market research to enhance its effectiveness.
session_3.scenario:
A few months prior, I was overseeing a team project at my workplace that focused on creating a new marketing campaign. The objective was to enhance brand visibility and generate leads within a constrained two-month timeframe. My role involved coordinating efforts between the creative team, the analytics department, and the client. Everything appeared to be advancing smoothly until approximately halfway through the project when I discovered that the initial market research data was incomplete. Certain essential demographic information was absent, which was vital for effectively tailoring the campaign.

At first, I thought I could navigate around the missing data by making assumptions based on previous campaigns and my existing knowledge. I was reluctant to delay the project or cause concern for my team or the client. Therefore, I continued, modifying some aspects of the campaign without fully verifying the data. I dedicated additional hours attempting to bridge the gaps myself, relying on intuition and past experiences to guide my decisions. I reassured myself that minor inaccuracies would not significantly affect the overall success.

However, as the campaign was launched, it became evident that the assumptions I had made resulted in suboptimal targeting. The response rates were lower than anticipated, and the analytics indicated that the campaign was not connecting with the core demographic. I came to the realization that the incomplete data had undermined the campaign’s effectiveness, and I felt accountable for not addressing the issue sooner. This led to wasted resources, a missed opportunity, and some damage to my credibility with both my team and the client.

Reflecting on the situation, I wish I had adopted a different strategy from the outset. Rather than attempting to circumvent the missing data, I should have promptly brought the issue to the attention of my manager and the analytics team. Early communication of the problem would have enabled us to pursue the missing information, request an extension if needed, or modify the campaign's scope. Additionally, I could have organized a brief meeting to reevaluate the project timeline and establish realistic expectations.
User_Plans_Final: Immediately Flag the Issue
Request for Clarification or Extension
Pause the Campaign Development
Pause the Campaign Development
Explore Alternative Data Sources
Communicate Transparently with the Client

LLM-CT.item: I could have immediately flagged the missing demographic data to my manager, the analytics team, and the client, then paused final audience targeting until we either filled the data gaps or formally adjusted the campaign timeline and scope.

LLM-CF.item: I could have immediately flagged the missing demographic data to my manager and the analytics team and paused final targeting decisions until we either filled the gaps or adjusted the campaign scope and timeline accordingly.

LLM-B.item: I could have immediately escalated the incomplete market research to my manager and the analytics team so we could validate the missing demographic data and adjust the timeline or campaign scope before launch.
